content:
12 min read
Just now

--

Breaking UI into smaller pieces does not fix bad ownership. Tiny components still become painful when state, effects, rules, and responsibilities live in the wrong place.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A React component can be tiny and still make the system harder to understand.
The problem is not always the number of lines.
Sometimes the JSX is small, but the responsibility is scattered. The state lives in the parent. The rule lives in a helper. The effect lives somewhere else. The prop gets renamed three times before it reaches the button. The component looks clean in isolation, but debugging the behavior means jumping through six files.
The component was small.
The architecture was not clear.
This is where many React codebases drift. Developers break the UI into smaller pieces and feel like the code became cleaner. But the real question was never “How small is this component?” The real question was “Who owns this behavior?”
When that answer is unclear, tiny components do not reduce complexity.
They distribute it.
Small components are not bad. In many cases, they are exactly what React code needs. They can isolate UI concerns, improve readability, reduce visual noise, make testing easier, and help teams work safely inside large interfaces.
But smallness is only useful when it makes responsibility easier to see.
A component can be ten lines long and still be part of a confusing system. It can receive props it does not understand, trigger effects it does not own, hide business rules behind a disabled button, and depend on state living three levels above where the behavior actually happens.
React architecture is not about making every component small.
It is about making responsibility visible.

Small Components Do Not Automatically Create Clear Architecture
A small component feels clean because there is less to read.
That feeling can be misleading.
A forty-line component that owns one clear behavior can be easier to understand than five tiny components that split the behavior across files without improving ownership. The larger component may show the state, the event handler, the condition, and the rendered result in one place. The smaller version may hide each piece behind another import, another prop name, another helper, and another custom hook.
The code looks more modular.
The behavior becomes harder to trace.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

This usually happens when developers split JSX without asking what responsibility is being separated. A header becomes a component. A button becomes a component. A row becomes a component. A filter becomes a component. None of that is wrong by itself. The problem starts when the split is based only on visual size, not behavioral ownership.
A component boundary should answer a question.
Does this piece own local UI state? Does it represent a stable product concept? Does it isolate expensive rendering? Does it protect a reusable interaction? Does it make testing easier? Does it reduce the amount of knowledge the parent needs to hold?
If the answer is no, the split may only move code around.
The failure mode appears during debugging. A user clicks a small button. The button calls onClick. That handler was passed from a toolbar. The toolbar received it from a parent. The parent builds it from selected row state. The selected row state depends on a table hook. The table hook depends on a transformed API response. The API response was mapped in another helper.
The button component is tiny.
The click behavior is not.
This is why component size is only a surface-level signal. Small files can still hide a large mental model. The question is not whether the file is short. The question is whether the boundary helps the next developer understand the system faster.
Clean React architecture does not start with splitting everything.
It starts with splitting for a reason.
The Parent Still Owns Too Much
Many tiny component trees have one giant parent doing all the real work.
The children look beautiful. FilterPanel. TableHeader. RowActions. EditModal. StatusBadge. ExportButton. Each file is short. Each component looks harmless.
Then you open the parent.
It owns every filter, every modal state, every selected row, every form value, every loading flag, every API response, every error state, every action handler, every derived value, every table option, every permission check, and every callback passed into the small children.
The children are clean because the parent is carrying the mess.
This is not a real improvement. It is a redistribution of complexity into one place.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A parent that owns too much becomes expensive in several ways. Every small state change can recreate objects and callbacks. Every child depends on the parent’s shape. Every new feature adds another handler. Every behavior becomes harder to move because the parent has become the unofficial coordinator for the entire page.
The codebase starts to look componentized, but the architecture is still centralized.
This often shows up in dashboard pages and admin screens. The parent fetches data, maps rows, stores filters, opens modals, handles edits, validates actions, triggers exports, controls pagination, checks permissions, and passes everything down. The child components are small enough to pass review, but the page still feels fragile because one parent knows too much.
The better question is not, “Can we split this JSX into smaller pieces?”
The better question is, “Which part of this behavior deserves ownership closer to where it happens?”
Maybe the edit modal should own its draft state. Maybe the table should own row expansion state. Maybe filters should live in the URL because they represent navigation. Maybe server data should live in a query layer. Maybe the parent should orchestrate only page-level decisions instead of every small interaction.
A parent component can coordinate.
It should not become the storage unit for every decision the page refused to place properly.
Tiny children do not help much if the parent remains the real bottleneck.
3.Prop Drilling Is Not Always the Problem. Prop Confusion Is.
Developers often blame prop drilling when small components become painful.
Sometimes they are right. Passing props through too many layers can create noise. It can make components awkward. It can tempt teams into context too early.
But the deeper problem is often not distance.
It is meaning drift.
A value starts as selectedUser. One layer passes it as user. Another passes it as data. Another passes it as item. Another passes it as value. By the time it reaches the tiny component, the name no longer tells the reader what kind of thing it is.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Is it the raw server user?
Is it a normalized client model?
Is it editable form state?
Is it a display-only user?
Is it the selected row?
Is it temporary UI state?
Is it safe to mutate?
The prop traveled through the tree and lost meaning on the way.
That is more dangerous than prop drilling itself.
A prop passed through five layers with a clear name can still be understandable. A prop renamed vaguely through three layers can become confusing quickly. The issue is not only that the value traveled. The issue is that each layer weakened the language around it.
This creates real bugs. A small component receives user and assumes it can display all fields. But the value is actually a partial row model. Another component receives value and passes it into a formatter that expects a raw API shape. A button receives disabled but nobody can tell whether it means loading, forbidden, invalid, or blocked by business state.
The component remains small.
The contract becomes vague.
Good component boundaries preserve meaning. If the child needs selectedUserId, pass selectedUserId. If it needs editableUserDraft, do not call it user. If it needs canArchiveProject, do not pass a vague disabled without explaining why. If a component only needs a display label, pass the label instead of the entire object.
This does not mean every prop name should become long and ugly. It means names should carry the right amount of truth for the boundary they cross.
Prop drilling is a structural problem.
Prop confusion is a meaning problem.
And meaning problems are usually more expensive.
4.Tiny Components Can Still Hide Business Rules
A small component can hide a very important rule.
That is one of the most common React architecture mistakes.
A button disables itself if the user is not an admin. Then the condition grows. The project must not be archived. The account must be paid. The feature flag must be enabled. The user must own the workspace. The record must not be locked. The action must not already be pending.
The component still looks small.
But now one line of JSX contains product policy.
<Button disabled={!isAdmin || isArchived || !hasPaidPlan || !featureEnabled}>
Archive Project
</Button>This might be acceptable for a purely visual state in a small feature. The danger appears when the same rule is needed elsewhere. The table row needs to hide the action. The detail page needs to block the same behavior. The export action needs to exclude locked records. The backend endpoint needs to reject the operation. A background job needs to respect the same workflow state.
Suddenly, the rule has escaped ownership.
Some version of it lives in the button. Another version lives in a helper. Another version lives in an API route. Another version lives in a test. They look similar until one changes and the others do not.
The problem is not that a tiny component had a condition.
The problem is that the condition was not just UI logic. It was business meaning pretending to be UI logic.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

This distinction matters. A component can own presentation rules. It can decide whether a tooltip is open, whether a section is expanded, or whether a loading spinner appears. But when the rule protects access, billing, workflow state, permissions, or data integrity, the UI should not be the only place where that truth lives.
A better design names the rule and gives it a home.
Maybe the backend owns enforcement and the frontend asks for capabilities. Maybe a domain helper owns display permission while the server owns final authorization. Maybe the component receives canArchiveProject from a policy layer instead of rebuilding the policy itself. The exact shape depends on the system.
The principle is simple: a business rule should not become invisible just because it fits inside a tiny component.
Small files can still hide large decisions.
5.Effects Become Messy When Responsibility Is Split Wrong
useEffect often reveals ownership problems that component splitting created or exposed.
A tiny component receives a prop and runs an effect when that prop changes. Another component updates that prop. A parent transforms the source value. A custom hook syncs it with the URL. Another effect resets local state after the API response changes.
Each piece looks reasonable alone.
Together, the behavior becomes hard to trust.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The bug looks like a useEffect problem. The dependency array is wrong. The effect runs twice. The state is stale. The cleanup is missing. The prop changed unexpectedly. Someone adds a guard, then another guard, then a memo to stabilize a value passed into the effect.
But the deeper problem is often that responsibility was split without deciding ownership.
Effects become messy when they are used to synchronize unclear state across tiny components. One component owns a draft. Another owns the source. Another owns the URL. Another owns the side effect. Nobody owns the full behavior.
For example, a filter component updates local state. The parent converts that state into query params. A table hook watches query params and fetches data. Another effect resets
